chapters.title: Getting to know your own feelings
chapters.text:
Strong feelings can sweep us off course and down roads we would rather avoid. If you can recognize those detours early, you can pick up new skills, build steadier self-control, and get to know your emotions in a clearer way. You can also stop yourself from acting on impulse in ways that hurt you or others.

Feelings carry real force, but with time and practice we can guide them instead of letting them guide us.

Emotional Intelligence 2.0 unpacks the idea of EQ, which is short for emotional intelligence, and lays it out in plain language. You get simple guidance along with exercises to try right away, so you can steadily raise your EQ to a new level.

The whole point of raising your EQ, which is also the point of this summary, is to feel more in command of yourself. On top of that, you will see gains across many parts of your life, and the road ahead starts to look a lot brighter.

EQ is not the same as IQ, the number that measures regular intelligence. Step through the rest of this piece with us and we will look at the many sides of EQ together.

chapters.title: What separates the winners from the rest
chapters.text:
History is full of people who aimed high and then surpassed their own targets. On the other hand, plenty of people have pushed hard toward a goal and never quite reached it. Why does that happen? We hear that effort can move mountains, and that trying hard enough can land you almost anything. Yet some people seem to find a way through, while others keep falling short.

We see this play out around us all the time, and a clean answer is hard to pin down. Still, Travis Bradberry and Jean Greaves argue we can narrow it down to each person's level of emotional intelligence. People with a higher EQ tend to do better, while those with a lower EQ often let feelings drive the bus, which keeps them from reaching what they want.

EQ can be a key divider between two people who score about the same on regular IQ tests.

Emotional intelligence is the skill of noticing and managing your own feelings, while also reading the feelings and situations of the people around you. There are many ways to raise your EQ, and this summary walks through those ways in depth.

One useful note: American psychologist Daniel Goleman named five parts of EQ, including self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skill.

chapters.title: Do your feelings run the show
chapters.text:
Every person feels things. It is a basic part of being human, yet feelings do not run everyone's day. People who manage their own reactions and stay aware of the reactions of others usually carry a higher EQ. That kind of steadiness is worth aiming for.

Humans wrestle with feelings because the brain has to sit with them before the reasoning part kicks in. A neurological process backs that up. Every bit of sensory input you take in, whether it is a smell, a taste, or a sound, races to your brain through electrical signals. Those signals pass through nerve cells and the spinal cord before they land in the brain. To make sense of them, the brain has to bring them up to the front, and on the way they have to slip through a region called the limbic system.

The limbic system is the part of the brain tied to feelings, so the moment you see or hear something, your body reacts with an emotion before reason has a chance to join the conversation. That is the electrical signal passing through the limbic system, sparking a feeling, and only then arriving at the part of the brain where logic takes the wheel.

The limbic system is closely linked to feelings and to how we react to them.

You have more say in this than you may think. The limbic system and the rational part of the brain work as a team, and the higher your EQ, the more in step they are. The lower your EQ, the more they trip over each other.

The big goal of Emotional Intelligence 2.0 is to lift your EQ so you can sidestep those snap calls and actions that come from raw emotion.

Feelings can lift you up or pull you down, but you do not really get a say in the matter until you understand them, as Travis Bradberry reminds us.

chapters.title: Why the EQ appraisal test matters
chapters.text:
Before you dive into the emotional intelligence strategies covered here, you need a clear starting point. That means taking the Emotional Intelligence 2.0 Appraisal Test.

Bradberry and Greaves pulled together some striking numbers from the results. They surveyed more than 500,000 people to see how big a role feelings play in everyday life. From that sample, only 36 percent could actually name the emotion they were feeling in the moment. Picture your temper starting to rise. You may not realize right away that you are angry. As a result, you are more likely to react in a way that does not serve you. That means our feelings end up steering about two-thirds of us, and that group tends to have a lower EQ.

Knowing how much of the wheel your feelings are grabbing will help you move forward. The path looks a little different for everyone.

Measuring your EQ can feel a bit like learning to waltz with a partner. If someone explains the dance first, you will probably pick up the basics and even feel the urge to try it yourself. If you then rehearse each step with a partner, your chances of remembering it on the dance floor climb fast.

So what can a higher EQ actually do for you? Bradberry and Greaves keep coming back to the idea that EQ is about noticing your own feelings and the feelings of others. What does that bring you in practical terms?

A higher EQ can lift the quality of your relationships, help you skip mistakes by making stronger choices, and raise the overall quality of your life.

Bradberry and Greaves also found that people with higher EQ tend to perform better at work, which can mean bigger paychecks. The gap can come out to about $29,000 more across a career than for someone with a lower EQ. That is a real difference, and it is well worth the effort to build your EQ.

As Travis Bradberry reminds us, anyone can fly into a temper. That part is easy. The hard part is aiming that temper at the right person, in the right measure, at the right time, for the right reason, and in the right way.

chapters.title: The four main EQ skills in plain English
chapters.text:
Emotional intelligence touches many corners of life, but this book zeroes in on four core skills. They sort into two buckets, personal and social.

Those four skills are personal self-awareness, personal self-management, social awareness, and relationship management.

By working on these four skills, you unlock the wider reach of a higher EQ. Friendships, romantic relationships, work relationships, and your career all stand to gain. You will also feel better about yourself, your confidence will grow, and you will handle everyday situations in a healthier and more level-headed way.

The simplest path to learning is repetition, which is a fancy way of saying practice makes perfect. Keep repeating the strategies in the book until your brain starts to reach for them automatically. Repetition works because it builds what scientists call brain plasticity.

To learn a new move, you have to repeat it until it feels like second nature.

Plasticity is the word for how the brain picks up new patterns and rewires itself. If you want to retrain your brain and shift your mindset, you have to lean on plasticity. The same applies as you work to raise your EQ.

The brain can grow, but it cannot get bigger because it sits inside the skull. It can, however, build more links between cells, and those new links in effect raise your ability to handle situations with more reason, which is the heart of a higher EQ. That kind of growth takes time, so give the practice room to breathe.

A useful fact: your ability to handle social and emotional situations counts about four times more than regular IQ when you are on the job.

chapters.title: Sharpening your self-management skills
chapters.text:
The next area to build is self-management. With steady self-management, you can understand and steer your emotions, sidestep rash choices, and decide what to say and do based on reason rather than feeling.

The ability to manage your feelings means you can make real decisions, and that lets you separate what is actually happening from how you feel about it.

A few simple habits can help you build self-management. Focus on your breathing when stress or pressure shows up. It helps you settle down and stop yourself from saying or doing something you will later wish you had not. Counting to ten can also help. Make lists that put your emotions and your reason side by side. That puts the moment in perspective and steers you toward a better call.

Share your goals out loud, so you feel a stronger pull to act on them. Take sleep seriously and aim for enough rest each night. Sleep helps you stay rational and act less on impulse. Build a positive outlook on life, and look for chances to smile and laugh. Carve out a small window each day to deal with problems before they pile up. Learn to interrupt overthinking and negative self-talk, and when you notice it happening, switch your mind to something more positive. Stop focusing on the things that limit you, and put your attention on what helps you move forward.

Look for a lesson in every person you meet. Make time for self-care and recharge your mental batteries. Accept that change is part of life, and stop fearing it.

A useful fact: there are four common shapes negative self-talk can take, including personalizing, polarizing, filtering, and exaggerating.

chapters.title: How socially aware are you
chapters.text:
The first piece of the social side of EQ is social awareness, the ability to pick up on the feelings and experiences of other people.

Picture a coffee shop where the barista has been extra friendly because she noticed you looked a little down. That barista is showing a high level of social awareness as part of her EQ.

Picking up on other people's feelings is a direct result of your social awareness.

Mindfulness meditation is a strong way to take in what is around you, and it also sharpens your focus on what is happening right now.

A few habits can help you build this skill. Greet the people you meet by name. Remember that body language is a big part of how we communicate, and it often tells you what words cannot. If you tend to scribble notes nonstop in meetings, try listening and watching instead. You will still take in plenty, and you may pick up more than you expected.

Declutter your life, including your living space, your mind, and any social circle that no longer serves you, along with items or people in your space that block you mentally and emotionally. Practice living in the present instead of jumping to a feared future or looking back in regret. Spend some time watching people. Head out for a coffee, sit down, and take in what is happening around you. Try to guess what a stranger may be feeling, or watch their body language for clues. Try to walk a mile in someone else's shoes. A high EQ goes hand in hand with a high level of empathy. Look for the full picture in every situation instead of reacting to small bits of information. Practice the art of real listening, because most of us hear words but few of us actually listen.

A useful fact: nearly half of marriages in the United States end in separation or divorce, so raising your EQ can help you steer clear of trouble.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on building your EQ
chapters.text:
Lifting your EQ will have a real effect on your life. By working on the strategies we have walked through, you will see your confidence grow, and that has a nice side effect of leaving you feeling more upbeat and more positive overall.

The first EQ skill sits in the personal bucket and is called self-awareness, which means understanding who you are at your core.

The biggest block to building self-awareness is the pull to avoid the discomfort that comes from seeing yourself as you really are, as Travis Bradberry points out.

You may think you know yourself, and in many ways you do. But do you really understand what drives you? Do you know your emotional triggers? Most people assume they have themselves figured out, but when they dig a little deeper, they often find surprises. To learn what really makes you tick, you have to know your emotional triggers.

A strong relationship takes time and steady effort. Not everyone understands that, and that gap in understanding can eventually break a relationship. The fallout can leave you with heartache and pain that hangs around for a long time.

The ability to keep relationships strong comes down to relationship management. When the early excitement of a partnership fades, keeping it alive takes work. Emotional intelligence can make that work feel much lighter.

A simple way to start is to take the Emotional Intelligence 2.0 Appraisal test and find your EQ starting point. Keep a journal of your feelings, noting how you felt on a given day and what may have sparked those feelings. That habit helps you spot your triggers. Spend an afternoon sitting in a public place, like a park, and just watch people. Try to guess what they may be feeling, and watch their body language for clues.
